content: The token generator service is responsible for generating, validating, and revoking stateful tokens using Redis as the storage backend. This service does NOT use JWT or any other stateless token format.
Dependencies: Redis adapter (implements Adapter interface from pkg/redis), Key (string): used as a secret for token generation (e.g., for HMAC or bcrypt).
Generate method behavior: Generate a new, unique, and unpredictable token string (recommended: use HMAC-SHA256 with key, or crypto/rand; bcrypt is possible but not standard for this use case).
Generate method behavior: Store the claims in Redis with the token as the key and claims as the value (JSON-encoded).
Generate method behavior: Set the Redis TTL to claims.EXP (expiration).
Generate method behavior: Return the generated token.
Validate method behavior: Retrieve the claims from Redis using the token as the key.
Validate method behavior: Check that the claims are valid (not before NBF, etc.).
Validate method behavior: Do NOT check claims.EXP; expiration is handled by Redis TTL (if the token is expired, it will not be found).
Validate method behavior: Return the claims if valid, or an error if not.
Revoke method behavior: Delete the token from Redis.
Best Practices: Use a secure, random, and unique token generator (HMAC, crypto/rand, or bcrypt if you have a specific reason).
Best Practices: Store claims as JSON in Redis.
Best Practices: Set TTL to claims.EXP for automatic expiration.
Best Practices: Ensure all methods are context-aware and return clear errors.
Best Practices: Define all error values as package-level variables/constants at the top of the file.
Best Practices: Do not expose Redis or token generation details in the public API.
Best Practices: Do NOT use JWT or any stateless token format.
Note: This service is designed for stateful token management using Redis. For stateless tokens, use a different approach.
